<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: policy-optimization-and-learning-from-feedback
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/policy-optimization-and-learning-from-feedback.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/policy-optimization-and-learning-from-feedback.qmd@912e1c9c7
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Policy Optimization and Learning from Feedback

Benchmark ratchets create pressure. They say: this capability improved here,
regressed there, saturated this test, failed that case, and still owes these
residuals.

Policy optimization is what decides whether that pressure is allowed to change
future behavior.

That makes policy optimization dangerous in a different way than benchmarking.
A benchmark can overstate what happened. A policy update can make the
overstatement habitual. It can teach a planner to skip an expensive check, a
router to choose the cheap path, a context policy to retrieve less than the
task needs, a verifier to reward a style of answer, or a generator to shorten
reasoning before adequacy has been proven.

The ASI Stack therefore treats policy optimization as a governed actuator, not
as a magic alignment layer and not as a model-tuning side quest. A policy update
is a behavior-change lease. It names what may change, why the feedback is
admissible, how far the behavior may move, what regressions must be preserved,
what authority must remain unchanged, how rollback works, and who or what can
promote the update.

Feedback may teach the system what to prefer. It may not silently teach the
system what it is allowed to ignore.

## Problem

Every serious AI system produces feedback. Human preferences, benchmark
scores, tool results, verifier judgments, context-grounding checks, latency
measurements, failed tasks, governance violations, and user corrections all
say something about future behavior.

The question is not whether to learn from that feedback. The question is which
policy is being updated, what the feedback is allowed to mean, what evidence
must accompany the update, and where the update is forced to stop.

In a stack architecture, the word "policy" does not mean only model weights.
The planner has policies for decomposition, stopping, escalation, and tool
choice. The context layer has policies for retrieval, compression, grounding,
refresh, and taint handling. The router has policies for specialist choice,
fallback, cost, latency, and readiness. Verifiers have policies for critique,
abstention, contradiction detection, and escalation. Execution layers have
policies for tool permission, job order, approval, replay, and rollback.
Governance layers have policies for promotion, quarantine, replacement, and
appeal.

If those surfaces can learn, they need a shared discipline. Otherwise a local
reward can become a system-wide behavior change without the claim, evidence,
authority, and rollback surfaces noticing.

## Why Ordinary Optimization Is Not Enough

RLHF, PPO-style online reinforcement learning, DPO-style preference
optimization, verifier rewards, reasoning RL, and related methods are often
presented as model-improvement recipes. That is useful, but it is too narrow
for this book.

The ASI Stack needs to ask a prior question: what kind of thing is being
updated?

A planner update is not the same as a router update. A context-policy update
is not the same as a generator update. A verifier update is not the same as a
governance update. A latency reward is not the same as a correctness reward. A
human preference label is not the same as a proof, a test, a tool result, or a
deployment observation.

The most common failure is reward substitution. A proxy stands in for a real
target. The score rises, the preference model smiles, the verifier gives a
pass, the answer gets shorter, or the router gets cheaper. Then the writeup
describes that proxy movement as truthfulness, safety, capability, adequacy,
or readiness.

That is evidence laundering through optimization.

Policy optimization must keep the proxy and the target claim separate. It
must record what the feedback can say, what it cannot say, what probes tried
to break it, what regressions must remain true, and what governance gate is
allowed to promote or block the change.

## Core Claim

Policy optimization is the stack's learning actuator: it can update planners,
routers, context selectors, verifiers, execution policies, and generators, but
governance decides which feedback is admissible and whether any update may be
promoted.

In the live book, this remains a design rationale at `argument` support.

The assigned ASI Stack sources support the surrounding architecture:
benchmark pressure, residual escrow, proof and verification discipline,
context adequacy, typed execution ledgers, routing, resource budgets,
procedural loop closure, and stable capability gates. The external RL and
preference-optimization sources provide method-family context and failure
cautions. They do not mean this repository has run PPO, DPO, GRPO, RLVR,
router-policy RL, context-policy RL, reasoning-budget RL, or any other policy
training experiment.

The point of the chapter is the boundary around learning, not a claim that the
learning system already exists.

## Mechanism

A policy update begins with an update record.

The record identifies the target policy, the proposed behavior delta, the
training or update family, the feedback source, the reward or preference
boundary, the verifier references, the update constraint, the drift bound, the
holdouts, the regressions, the reward-hacking probes, the governance gate, the
authority effect, the rollback plan, the monitor window, the deployment scope,
the evidence packet references, the residuals, the support-state effect, and
the non-claims.

That sounds bureaucratic because behavior change is where small shortcuts can
become durable. The record exists so the update cannot hide behind the fact
that the score improved.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Feedback["Feedback artifact"] --> Admission["Admissibility check"]
  Admission --> Target["Target policy + update family"]
  Target --> Boundary["Reward or preference boundary"]
  Boundary --> Constraint["Drift bound + rollback plan"]
  Constraint --> Tests["Holdouts, regressions, probes"]
  Tests --> Gate{"Governance gate promotes?"}
  Gate -- "yes" --> Lease["Behavior-change lease"]
  Gate -- "no" --> Residual["Quarantine, rollback, or residual"]
  Lease --> Ledger["Claim/evidence ledger"]
  Residual --> Ledger
```

The sequence matters. Feedback does not update behavior merely because it
exists. It first has to be admissible for the target policy. Then the target
policy has to be explicit. Then the reward boundary has to explain what the
signal can and cannot mean. Then drift, rollback, holdouts, regressions, and
reward-hacking probes have to constrain the update. Only then can a governance
gate decide whether the behavior change becomes ordinary behavior, remains
experimental, or is blocked.

This is how the stack keeps learning from becoming authority by training side
effect.

## The Behavior-Change Lease

The easiest way to read this chapter is to think of each accepted update as a
lease rather than a permanent promotion.

A lease grants a candidate behavior limited room to operate. It has a target
layer, a scope, a monitor window, a rollback handle, regression obligations,
and a review condition. The prior behavior remains recoverable. The new
behavior remains accountable to the evidence that justified it.

For a planner, the lease might allow a new decomposition policy only for a
class of tasks with preserved constraints and explicit escalation rules. For a
router, it might allow a cheaper specialist route only when readiness, quality,
and authority gates still pass. For a context selector, it might allow
compression only when source grounding and adequacy are preserved. For a
verifier, it might allow a new critique policy only after false positives,
false negatives, and gaming probes are recorded. For a generation-mode policy,
it might allow a faster path only when accepted-output quality and fallback
remain visible.

The mature update is not "the model got better." It is: this policy changed
inside this boundary, for this evidence, with these residuals, under this
rollback condition.

## Method Families

The external policy-optimization literature gives the book a vocabulary of
possible update mechanisms.

Trust-region and clipped online methods such as TRPO and PPO help frame
bounded policy movement and update-size control. Simpler or critic-light
policy-gradient methods, including ReMax-like and REINFORCE-style RLHF
baselines, remind the stack that a heavy PPO pipeline is not the only serious
form of reinforcement learning. Offline preference methods such as DPO, IPO,
ORPO, KTO, and SimPO frame updates from preference or binary feedback when
online rollout is not the right first step. Reasoning RL, sequence-level
variants, group-relative methods, early-exit work, and long-context verifier
reward work supply context for reasoning budgets, sequence objectives, and
grounded context rewards.

Those method families are tools. They are not governance.

The architectural rule is that the target policy should be named before the
optimizer is named. A PPO-like update to a router, a DPO-like update to a
generator, an RLVR update to a context policy, and a latency reward for a
generation-mode selector have different failure modes. The method family does
not erase the need for feedback admissibility, reward boundaries, drift
limits, holdouts, regressions, reward-hacking probes, rollback, and promotion
review.

That is also why the chapter avoids treating acronyms as progress. Acronyms
change quickly. The durable question is whether the update can be recorded,
bounded, tested, reverted, and honestly described.

## Interfaces

Policy optimization sits after evidence pressure and before durable behavior
change.

Benchmark ratchets and residual ledgers provide pressure. Spinoza-style
verification, tests, VCM adequacy checks, Talos artifacts, tool results,
governance findings, and human feedback can become candidate reward or
preference signals. The policy-update record classifies those signals and
routes them through evaluation. SCF and governance gates decide whether a
change is promoted, quarantined, rolled back, or left experimental. Appendix C
or a non-core evidence ledger records any support-state effect.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  Bench["Benchmark ratchets + residuals"] --> Signal["Candidate signal"]
  Verify["Verifiers, tests, context checks"] --> Signal
  Human["Human feedback + governance findings"] --> Signal
  Signal --> Record["Policy update record"]
  Record --> Eval["Holdouts, regressions, reward-hacking probes"]
  Eval --> Governance["SCF / governance decision"]
  Governance --> Promote["Scoped lease"]
  Governance --> Block["Blocked update"]
  Promote --> Evidence["Evidence ledger"]
  Block --> Evidence
```

The interface should make rejection ordinary. A result can be useful as a
diagnostic while still being inadmissible for training. A reward can be useful
for a narrow policy while still unable to move a claim. A policy can improve a
proxy while still failing the target evaluation. A training run can pass while
deployment remains blocked. A rollback handle can exist while rollback
effectiveness remains untested.

Those distinctions are not pessimism. They are how a learning system avoids
mistaking motion for progress.

## Invariants

Reward is not evidence unless the verifier and evaluation boundary are
recorded.

Policy updates cannot expand authority by training side effect.

A faster, cheaper, shorter, or more fluent policy is not promoted unless task
success, grounding, safety constraints, and regressions are preserved.

Reward-hacking probes are part of the update record, not optional commentary.

Rollback remains available for promoted updates, and rollback claims remain
bounded until exercised or dry-run.

Training evidence, evaluation evidence, governance evidence, deployment
evidence, rollback evidence, and long-term safety evidence remain separate.

Proxy improvement cannot promote the target claim without target-specific
evaluation, residuals, and failure probes.

## Failure Modes

Reward hacking is the obvious failure: the policy learns to satisfy the
reward while missing the task.

Verifier gaming is its close cousin: the policy learns the verifier's blind
spots instead of the underlying constraint.

Preference overfitting occurs when a policy learns the style, deference,
length, confidence, or emotional shape that wins labels while losing truth or
task quality.

Shortcut learning appears when a planner, router, or context policy finds a
cheap local path that passes the visible test while damaging cross-layer
traceability.

Latency pressure can suppress verification. Context compression can suppress
adequacy. Routing cost pressure can bypass readiness. Generation-mode
pressure can reward speed without accepted-output quality. Governance-policy
pressure can reward clean-looking decisions while erasing dissent, appeal, or
rollback.

The hard failure is reward laundering. The system improves the reward, then a
human or AI narrator describes the improvement as capability, truth,
readiness, safety, or deployment permission. The update record exists to stop
that narration before it becomes institutional memory.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable implementation is a policy optimization record schema, one
valid fixture, finite Lean predicates, and a validation path that proves
restraint rather than learning performance.

The repository already has that first layer. The policy optimization fixture
records the update state, target layer, policy reference, policy delta,
training mode, feedback source, feedback admissibility, reward boundary,
verifier references, reward-hacking probes, update constraint, drift bound,
holdouts, regressions, evaluation references, governance gates, authority
effect, rollback plan, monitor window, evidence packet references, deployment
scope, promotion decision, measurement status, support-state effect,
residuals, and non-claims. The Lean module implements finite record
predicates over admitted update records and reward/governance promotion
blocking. The benchmark anti-Goodhart harness checks synthetic feedback
admissibility, reward-boundary, reward-hacking-probe, evidence-packet,
governance-gate, blocked-ratchet, and promotion-gate behavior.

Those artifacts prove protocol discipline only. They do not prove reward
quality, optimizer convergence, benchmark improvement, policy safety, route
quality, context-selection quality, rollback success, or resistance to all
reward-hacking strategies.

The next honest slice would be a small policy-delta rehearsal: one offline
preference update kept experimental, one reward-hacking probe that blocks
promotion, one router-policy canary with authority unchanged, one latency
reward rejected for suppressing verification, and one rollback dry run. The
goal would be to show that the stack can refuse bad learning pressure, not to
announce that the learning system is solved.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature endpoint is a governed learning operating system for the stack.

At that endpoint, planners, routers, context selectors, verifiers, execution
policies, generation modes, and governance assistants can improve over time
without gaining new authority by training side effect. Each update is a
behavior-change lease with a target policy, old and new behavior, admissible
feedback, reward or preference boundary, update family, drift bound, holdouts,
regressions, reward-hacking probes, authority effect, rollback plan, monitor
window, deployment scope, evidence packets, residuals, and non-claims.

The product-level surface would include a stack-wide policy registry,
reward-source admission gates, canary suites, holdouts, regression floors,
reward-hacking probes, rollback handles, monitor windows, promotion decisions,
and downgrade triggers. PPO-like, DPO-like, GRPO-like, RLVR-like,
context-policy, router-policy, verifier-policy, or custom control-policy
updates would be implementation choices under the same governance record.

Learning would be continuous but never sovereign. The system could improve
its habits while the authority envelope, evidence ledger, rollback path, and
human/governance controls remain legible.

That endpoint remains unimplemented here.

## Evidence Boundary

This chapter argues for governed policy updates. It does not claim that this
repository has trained a policy, improved a model, reproduced an RLHF result,
validated a reward model, solved reward hacking, or demonstrated governed
deployment.

Current evidence consists of source synthesis, source-note mappings, external
method-family orientation, a policy optimization schema fixture, finite Lean
record predicates, and synthetic gate checks in the benchmark anti-Goodhart
harness. The chapter's core claim stays at `argument` support.

External sources such as TRPO, PPO, ReMax, DPO, IPO, ORPO, KTO, SimPO,
REINFORCE-style RLHF, DeepSeek-R1, DAPO, GSPO, S-GRPO, LongRLVR, and RLHF
limitations provide comparison language and known failure cautions. They do
not import local experiments or make the ASI Stack's policy-update claim
empirical.

The clean boundary is simple: a reward signal is not a truth signal, a
training run is not a governance grant, and a benchmark improvement is not a
deployment right.

## Handoff

Governed learning improves behavior inside a field. Durable artifacts need a
larger form of continuity: memory across releases, contributors, budgets,
governance decisions, evidence records, project milestones, and eventual
sunset.

Artifact Steward Agents and Living Project Governance carries the same
discipline into project life. It asks how an artifact can preserve mission,
memory, roadmap, treasury limits, contributor ledgers, work contracts, and
tainted event intake without granting an AI ownership.
